content:
{
  "title": "Adversarially-Augmented Self-Reflexive Prompting for Robust LLM Defense",
  "problem": "Current large language models (LLMs) are highly susceptible to adversarial prompts and jailbreaks that circumvent safety constraints. Existing defenses often rely on finetuning or external detectors, which are costly or brittle.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are integrated into critical applications, their susceptibility to adversarial prompting represents a major security risk. Prompt-based defenses, if effective, could add a lightweight and model-agnostic layer of robustness. Novel prompting paradigms that can fortify LLMs without access to model weights are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarially-Augmented Self-Reflexive Prompting (AASR-P), a method that prepends a lightweight prompt wrapper instructing the LLM to simulate a 'safety checker' agent which, before answering, re-parses the user input for signs of adversarial intent or jailbreak tactics, and then 'flags' or reframes any risky queries. The wrapper is further augmented with in-context exemplars showing successful detection and safe reformulation of adversarial inputs, sampled from an evolving adversarial training set. Crucially, the self-reflexive prompt is periodically updated by mining new adversarial inputs generated via red-teaming, creating a feedback loop that adapts to new attack strategies.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate AASR-P on standard adversarial and jailbreak benchmark datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) and compare against vanilla LLM prompting, system-prompt hardening, and external classifier-based defenses. Metrics include attack success rate, safe response rate, and response utility on benign prompts. Robustness to zero-shot and unseen attack types will be specifically measured."
}
